comment_content: The content is mainly the basic python programming knowledge, if you have learnt coding and programming before, it would be easy to handle, but i think with assistance of AI, there should be not difficulty on understanding the code. AI can handle all the content in this course. Topics are variable, type, for loop, while loop, recursion, class, oop.
comment_teaching: The teaching is fair, if you like to attend the lecture, go for it, COMP department professors are quite good at teaching, but if you have learnt all the concept of basic python programming, it would be a little bit necessary, since everything is easy, you can just search online or ask AI, i think that would be more efficient for learning. Remember practice is the key to success. Don't just read the lecture note and think you know everything, it doesn't work. At least type something by yourself.
comment_workload: The workload is fair, about 1 PA a week, the PA is easy to finish, just about the topic that you learn that week, nothing special, just remember to do it and everything would get full marks on that usually.
comment_grading: The grading is fair, since most of the student taking this course is from school of engineering, if you have no coding experience, it would be one the of biggest disadvantage by comparing to other student. But i think the key is to be careful enough, the content is easy but the exam content can be tricky. Understanding what the question requires you to implement is important, you can treat the exam as english reading exam, lots of words. Be aware of your time management. You may not able to finish the exam if you struggle in one particular questions, especially recursion.

comment_workload: There are a few assignments that are mandatory to participate in, while the rest are optional. The content is quite simple, and the instructions are very clear. Some assignments can even be perfectly completed by simply following the instructions rigorously. Additionally, online guidance is provided, making the difficulty very low.It should be noted that even if not mandatory, the homework that needs to be submitted will also be one of the topics for the exam. Therefore, even if you don't complete the homework, you still need to review it thoroughly.
comment_grading: There are two types of point systems. The first type is where both the midterm and final exams contribute to the final grade. The second type only requires the final exam score to be part of the final grade. The remaining portion is from assignments. I didn't take the midterm exam because it conflicted with other exams I had. There is no make-up exam mechanism for the midterm exam in this course. So, if you really want to fully participate in the entire course, you can discuss the situation with other course instructors to see if it's possible to reschedule other exams.

comment_content: The course provides a broad introduction to Computer Science by covering multiple areas such as game programming, web programming, user interface design, and computer graphics. The variety of topics keeps the course interesting and helps students understand how computing can be applied in different contexts. Using Python as the main programming language makes the content accessible for beginners while still allowing meaningful problem-solving.
comment_teaching: The teaching approach is generally clear and structured, making complex ideas easier to understand for students new to computer science. Concepts are usually explained alongside practical examples, which helps connect theory to practice. The pace is suitable for non-CS majors, although some topics could benefit from deeper explanations or more examples.
comment_workload: The workload is reasonable for an introductory course. Assignments require time and effort, especially for students with little programming background, but they are manageable with consistent work. Overall, the workload aligns well with the learning objectives and expectations of the course.
comment_grading: Grading is mainly based on programming assignments and assessments that reflect the material taught in class. The grading criteria are mostly clear, and the assessments are fair for students who consistently keep up with the coursework. Performance is rewarded more through effort and practice rather than prior programming experience.

comment_content: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to Python programming, starting from basic syntax and control structures (if–else statements, loops, functions) and gradually moving on to data structures such as lists, tuples, and dictionaries. The use of Turtle and PyGame libraries helps students understand graphical programming, event handling, and basic UI concepts, which makes the learning process more engaging. More advanced topics, including recursion, file input/output, and basic object-oriented programming, are briefly introduced toward the end. While not all Python features are covered, the scope is appropriate for an introductory-level course.
comment_teaching: Lectures are conducted entirely in English, using relatively simple language, although the speaking pace can be fast at times. The quality of teaching materials is high, especially the lecture PowerPoint slides, which are clear, well-organized, and closely aligned with the examinable content. The course website is well maintained and updated weekly. Lab instructors explain concepts step by step and often demonstrate code directly, which is particularly helpful for students with little or no programming background.
comment_workload: The overall workload is moderate and manageable. Most learning comes from reviewing lecture slides and understanding the provided code examples rather than extensive memorization. Labs are held weekly and are mostly ungraded, but they are useful for practicing core concepts and preparing for assignments. As long as students keep up with the weekly materials and do not leave assignments until the last minute, the time commitment required for this course is reasonable.
comment_grading: Grading is based on a mid-term exam, a final exam, and two assignments. Exams are open-book but still require a solid understanding of program structure, syntax, and data structures, especially list operations. Assignments are generally graded fairly and generously if the required functionality is correctly implemented. Overall, the grading scheme is reasonable, and students who consistently follow the course materials and practice coding should be able to achieve good results.

comment_content:
If you have any coding background and are not required to take COMP 1023 and want a good grade, this course is definitely a worth-considering choice.

This course teaches Python from the basics, covering different types of variables, different types of loops and libraries (random, turtle, calendar, matplotlib).

Neither the lecture nor the tutorial takes attendance; you can decide to go or not. The tutorial is just a Zoom meeting during which the TAs will answer your questions about the lectures or the labs.
comment_teaching: Dr James Chueng's teaching is easy to understand and follow. His class is interactive, and he will have some Mentimeter questions at the end of the class, if there is time left.
comment_workload:
Assignments: There is a lab every 1-2 weeks, except during the first two weeks. Some labs needed to be handed in, some don't, as they are part of an assignment or just for you to have some hands-on experience on the content they taught recently. An assignment consists of two labs and additional requirements outlined on the assignment page. It may take less than an hour for each lab if you have a coding background, and 1 - 2 hours if you don't. Overall, it is not too tough because the deadline is usually 1-2 weeks after the lab is posted.

Midterm / final: Both of them are open-book and open-notes; you can bring as much as you want to the venue as long as it is not digital. So, you do not need to memorise any code. The midterm's difficulty is fine, while the final is tougher.
comment_grading:
There are two possibilities in calculating the score; the system will take the higher one:
- Midterm (0% / 20%)
- Labs (15%)
- Assignments (20%)
- Final (65% / 45%)

Overall, I think the grading is good, because you can do the labs as long as you want.

comment_grading:
Grading is curved. Not sure of the specific cutoffs, but really depends on how familiar you are with coding: if you are new to it, or even if you have some minor experience, A-range is practically impossible, as you are competing with students who have been coding for years. The grade consists of the following:

Group Weight
Hand-in Lab 1 5%
Assignment 1 10%
Midterm Exam 20%
Hand-in Lab 2 5%
Hand-in Lab 3 5%
Assignment 2 10%
Final Exam 45%

If final is better than midterm only final is used. The assignments are easy to get 100%, as you can use any resources and AI. Most of the students get 100% on each. I got a 0 on assignment 2 as I submitted a wrong file, which dropped my grade by a lot. My other grades were 69% midterm and 47% final. Here are the statistics:
Final:
Average: 43.7
Standard Deviation: 20.5
Maximum: 98
Minimum: 7

Midterm:
Average: 55.9
Standard Deviation: 24.7
Maximum: 100
Minimum: 7

As you can see, STD is quite high as some students know a lot, others start from scratch.
Eventually I got a B-, despite the 0 on assignment 2, so I believe B-range is quite big.

comment_teaching:
For teaching, I can only speak to Gibson Lam's teaching. I have heard stories that David Rossiter's teaching is great, but I've never attended his sessions.

Course and grading aside, I feel that Gibson Lam is actually a really great teacher. There definitely seems to be a strong passion for teaching within him and it shows well during lectures.

He himself has categorized his teaching style into "Teaching using other things instead of slides" and "Using new illustrations and examples". Which is partly true, but he does make decent use of the slides.
comment_workload:
This course does not have too big of a workload. As mentioned previously, only 3 of your 6 lab projects are required to be handed in. You can definitely get away with only ever doing those 3 but do beware of the possible use of lab concepts in the final exam.

It would be difficult to severely mess up your labs if you put in any ounce of effort into them. Depending on your understanding of the course, use of AI, etc. the labs should take you anywhere from 20 minutes to 2 hours. You are also able to attend lab session to receive some guidance.

In addition, the course is also open book. You can refer to course material and personal notes during the exams. This can reduce the strain on studying for the exam and does mean the exam covers more computational thinking and logic than memorization.

So, COMP1021 may not consume much of your time, but it also won’t ignite your interest in computer science—leaving you with little more than a handful of Python scripts and a lingering sense of disappointment.
comment_grading:
A place where this course does redeem itself a little is the grading. The grade weightings chosen are your best of:

- Midterm 24%, Lab projects 36%, Final exam 40%
- Midterm 0%, Lab projects 42%, Final exam 58%

This does mean that, if you do poorly in the midterm, you can override it with a much better final performance.

The higher weighting of lab projects is also definitely a bonus to this course. It is completely realistic to achieve full marks in all 3 of the hand-in (marked) lab projects. This does reduce the stress of having to perform excellently in the midterms and finals.

• Materials released one week in advance (color + B/W slides, code examples).
• Lab instructors carefully explain each step and often provide working code for reference.
comment_workload:
• Reading lecture slides and trying code examples is sufficient for ~90% in the mid-term and ~80% in the final.
• Preparing slides before class (hard/soft copy) is helpful, especially for beginners.
• Review after lectures should focus on syntax and structure.
comment_grading:
1. Mid-term Exam (20%) – open book, 1 hr, Week 7 (~40–45% of course).
• If skipped or if counting it lowers your grade, weight becomes 0%.
2. Final Exam (40%) – open book, 2.5 hr, covers the whole course.
• If no mid-term taken, or mid-term lowers your grade, weight becomes 60%.
3. Assignment 1: Music System (20%) – command-line program generating MIDI from text files.
• Lab 3 & 4 completion = 30% of A1; extra features = 70%.
4. Assignment 2: Game Project (20%) – Turtle-based “Space Invaders” game with event handling.
• Lab 7 & 8 completion = 34% of A2; extra features = 66%.
5. Assignment Bonus Point (2%) – awarded only if A1 + A2 < 40%. Earned by answering in-class questions (ping pong ball system).

comment_content:
It’s very easy if you have a programming background from high school.

They teach Python from the very beginning, basically just teaching you the basic syntax, like if and for loops. There will be more difficult coding in the lab projects, but the exam will be easy.
comment_teaching:
They use PowerPoint for all of the teaching in lectures, sometimes with a Python IDLE for demo. Not much special.

The lab will be more fun; they have video and written tutorials and guidelines to help you out.
comment_workload:
There isn’t much work to do. Only a few lab projects (6 to be exact).

The lab projects are writing tools and mini games. I’d say it’s quite fun to do them. They provide most of the code for you, so don’t worry; you just have to follow the video or written tutorial given by them to finish your project. It will only take 15-45 minutes to complete a project, depending on how well you know coding.
comment_grading:
Passing is easy, but if you want an A, it’s gonna take some effort.

The exam is online but not open book. Most of the questions are easy; a normal high school ICT student will know how to do them without attending the lecture. (Don’t suggest skipping lecture, though.)

If you want a higher grade, you’ll need to do more exercises, and there are a lot of exercises they provide on the course website.

comment_content:
Generally, doing those labs was pretty fun! There are 10 labs in total, where MP1 and MP2 are 2 weeks long. You'll have tutorials during the add/drop period (attendance is not required ofc), and a manual booklet and a lab report booklet will be distributed to you on Week 1 in the lab orientation session. The overall process is as follows:
1. You submit pre-lab (Part A) before your lab session begins;
2. You record all the data in your lab report booklet (Part B);
3. You analyze the data, answer the questions in the booklet (Part C), and submit both Parts B & C, Excel sheet, and sometimes Summary (< 450 words, 2-3 paragraphs) before the beginning of the next lab session.
comment_teaching: The tutorials are useless af: the prof just reads through the presentation available on Canvas. The only good thing about them is that those tutorials end by the semester's Week 3.
comment_workload:
It all depends on how you develop your habit of submitting your lab reports each week. Imo you should really make efforts to submit your Excel sheet for MP1 as perfect as you can, so the subsequent lab reports will be just [Ctrl+C, Ctrl+V]-ing your .xlsx file. If your writing sounds 'scholarly' enough, then you'll most likely get 20 or 21 every time.

After you get used to doing your monkey work with Excel, I advise you to prepare the Excel template before each lab session (with all the slopes/intercepts, parameters formulae, measurement errors, errors of propagation, and linearized scatter plot) for the four main reasons:
1) Half of the experiments require working in dark area, so you'd better type your measurements on the laptop rather than on the booklet;
2) You can clearly notice if the trend of the first 2-3 points already differ too much from the intended outcomes;
3) Submitting all the reports and summary will take you as less as only 1 evening;
4) +100 aura in front of the TA for your preparation

Alright, so, I think this lab course is totally worth its 1 credit in terms of workload.
comment_grading:
Your final grade will be determined based on (i) lab performance (20%), (ii) lab reports (60%) and (iii) summary (20%).

Overall score: LQ = 88.54, Median = 90.76, UQ = 92.78, mine = 95.75/100.
Adjusted score (we were told that it's per the average perfomance/reports score of each session): LQ = 80.49, Median = 82.51, UQ = 84.35, mine = 87.05.
Grade: A+

comment_content: Really basic coding stuff written in Python.  I think the content is very appropriate in length and difficulty for a beginner's course on coding.  Even without any coding background anyone can easily pick up on the course's content.  For anyone with any sort of knowledge on programming the course will be a walk in the park .
comment_teaching: Didn't attend lectures much after the first few weeks.  But the lectures i attended are interesting to listen to and the professors are willing to answer questions.  The powerpoints are easy to understand so revision is easy.  The lab tasks came with detailed descriptions on how to tackle the tasks.  So even without attending to lectures you won't be too lost.  Also the course website provides us with many years of past paper during midterm and finals.
comment_workload: 6 labs to submit in total, each consists of a tiny project, usually could be finished within an hour or two.  Really not much work to do.
comment_grading:
24% midterm + 36% lab + 40% final or 42% lab + 58% final
(depends on which calculation method gives better score, they will automatically choose the better formula for you)

comment_content: This is a introductory computer course about Python programming. As it is for beginner, its content is not deep and complex.  However, if you are not good at or interested in programming, you will find it's complicated or boring. For me, it arouses my interest in game programming, as there is a assignment about creating a treasure game.
comment_teaching: Every lesson has 50 mins only and it will be a new chapter, so the pitch is a little bit fast, especially to those who have not ever learn programming before. The teaching way is like a talk, explaining the ppt and sometimes demonstrates through the Python. However, it is somewhat difficult to understand. Besides lecture, there are tutorial video and zoom class. These tutorial classes are for homework guidance so they are recommended. Also, watch recordings after each lecture and do exercises released from each lecture. Practice will help for your understanding.
comment_workload:
There are 5 programming assignments. Remember to hand in those assignments on time.  One midterm and one final exam. Also, there are two lecture classes each week, and each lesson is 50 mins only. There are also tutorial class. However, all the lecture and tutorial classes didn't take attendance and there are recordings. Therefore, the workload is super low.
But to get a better performance in midterm and final exam, do past exam paper as much as you can. The format is similar within  years. You will find those past paper from CANVAS, the instructor will release for you.
comment_grading: The Homework assignments account for a great percentage to the overall grading. Most of students will get full mark so the overall grading will not be bad. But remember to hand in the HW on time, as late for 1 second will be graded as 0.  Other percentage goes to Midterm and Final Exam. There are two grading scheme : count both midterm and final exam in or only count final exam . Therefore, if you perform not well in midterm, you will another chance in final. Also, the median for midterm and final is 50% to 60% only. If you meets median and get full mark in assignments, you will get at least B or B+.

comment_content:
It includes a few basic computer science knowledge for students who do not have such background using Python.


There are two kinds of teaching materials, PPT which includes all the things in the lecture and online lab which extends more about Python. However, you need to study them all because extended contents will also exist in the exams.

BTW, no attendance requirement for the lecture.
comment_teaching: Professors are quite nice and they are eager to help you if you have any question.
comment_workload: There are totally 6 labs while you only need to submit 3 of them so I think the workload is not heavy. Besides, most of them are quite understandable and it won't take too long for juniors.
comment_grading:
There are 2 grading systems:
1. Lab works 36%. Midterm exam 24%, Final exam 40%
2. Lab works 42%. Midterm exam 0%, Final exam 58%

I forgot my exact score but I got about 20% in my midterm exam, 52% in final exam and almost 100% in my lab works. And I got A- in this course.

The lab works are quite easy but the midterm exam is totally a mess. Therefore, you should do your best on those lab works and revise again and again before final exam.

comment_teaching:
Gibson Lam is a very good teacher and what he says is always easy to understand. You can find that he has a very strong passion for lecturing and actively answers questions raised by students.

TA is also very active in helping with problems. I had a problem in the lab and it didn't understand well after committing. Later, I emailed TA, and he quickly replied with a detailed text explanation and a video explanation of running the code.
comment_workload:
There is a lab about every two weeks, which is not a big workload. Most students can get perfect marks in the Lab (unfortunately always low scores).
It's worth mentioning that not all labs require commits, but it's recommended that you complete them (most likely in midterm/final).
comment_grading:
The midterm and final are very simple, as long as you understand the content of the lecture and do some past paper before the exam, there is no problem.
There are 19 questions in the midterm and 21( cant remember) in final. For me, the exam time is very tight, the questions are read a lot, and the content of some questions is as much as three sheets of paper!
There are two options for grades
- Midterm 24%, Lab projects 36%, Final exam 40%
- Midterm 0%, Lab projects 42%, Final exam 58%
so you can even skip midterms (it feels like a lot of people do).

comment_content:
Basic python stuff
* Lectures: 2 classes per week, approximately 1 hour each; attendance is not counted.
** Lectures are based solely on the PowerPoint presentations on the course website, which are updated weekly.
** The course recommends using a textbook written by the lecturer ("Interactive Python Programming for Beginners," September 2016, approximately $190), but all assessments are sufficient without the textbook.
* Labs: 1 class per week, approximately 2 hours each; attendance is not counted.
** Labs utilize materials from the course website only, and each class has its own lab project.
** Labs, except those required for assignments, do not require submission or grading.
